questions.question: How do you measure success of the Hot Home feature in Redfin?

saraswati.real_test: The interviewer is testing whether you understand that measuring success of a prediction feature requires evaluating the prediction itself, not just user engagement with it.
saraswati.senior_distinction: A mid-level PM proposes engagement metrics. A senior PM starts by asking what the prediction accuracy is and whether it's being tracked at all — then builds the measurement framework from accuracy upward.
takeaways: For prediction features, measure accuracy of the prediction before engagement with it — engagement without accuracy is a vanity metric.
Separate stakeholder success metrics — buyer utility and seller satisfaction can conflict, and blending them hides the tension.
Measure trust decay through repeat behaviour — trust erodes slowly then breaks suddenly.

episodes.title: Measuring Redfin's Hot Home: Start Here, Not There
episodes.subtitle: Before you pick a metric, you need to understand what the feature actually claims — and why that changes everything.
episodes.youtube_description: Most PMs jump straight to click-through rate and tour bookings when asked to measure Redfin's Hot Home feature. This episode shows why that's the wrong starting point — and what question you should ask first about any prediction feature.

interactive.intro: The way you answer this question reveals how you think about prediction features. Make a choice at each step — the reasoning unlocks after you commit.

decision_points.prompt: Before defining any metrics — what's the first thing you'd want to know about the Hot Home feature?

options.label: How many users are clicking Hot Home listings vs non-Hot listings
options.feedback_reveal: Click-through rate tells you the badge draws attention. It doesn't tell you whether the badge is worth acting on. A visible but unreliable badge can have great CTR right up until users stop trusting it. Engagement is a proxy for attention, not value.

options.label: Whether homes labelled Hot are actually selling faster than unlabelled ones
options.feedback_short: Prediction accuracy first — that's the senior move.
options.feedback_reveal: Before any engagement metric makes sense, you need to know if the prediction is correct. Hot Home claims a home will sell quickly. The ground truth is whether it did. If the accuracy is 40%, every engagement metric is measuring how effectively you're misleading buyers. Accuracy is the foundation — everything else layers on top.

options.label: Who decided what 'Hot' means and whether that definition should be questioned
options.feedback_short: Questioning the definition — that's staff-level thinking.
options.feedback_reveal: What exactly makes a home 'Hot'? Is it the algorithm's confidence score? Time-to-offer historically? The definition of Hot determines what the feature can ever measure. If the definition is fuzzy, the feature will never have a clean success metric. Staff PMs question the premise before measuring anything.

decision_points.prompt: You've established that accuracy matters. Whose success are you measuring — the feature serves multiple stakeholders.

options.label: Buyers primarily — they're the ones acting on the badge to schedule tours faster
options.feedback_reveal: Buyers are the primary actors on the Hot Home signal. But sellers also have a stake in the label — a seller whose home is not marked Hot may list with a competitor. If you ignore seller perception, you'll miss a churn signal that shows up in listing supply, not buyer behaviour.

options.label: Both buyers and sellers — and their success metrics can conflict with each other
options.feedback_short: Both stakeholders, with the tension named — that's the senior answer.
options.feedback_reveal: More homes labelled Hot makes sellers happier and reduces listing churn. But it dilutes the signal for buyers and reduces prediction accuracy. A senior PM makes this tension explicit and proposes measuring both sides with the understanding that optimising one can hurt the other.

options.label: Redfin's transaction rate is the only metric that ultimately matters — the others are proxies
options.feedback_short: Transaction rate as north star — provocative and partially right.
options.feedback_reveal: Redfin makes money on closed transactions. Ultimately, if Hot Home drives more completed deals faster, it's succeeding. But this is a lagging metric — you only see it after months. A staff PM uses transaction rate as the north star while building leading indicators to detect success or failure faster.

decision_points.prompt: What's the metric most likely to tell you the feature is failing — before it shows up in transaction data?

options.label: Declining click-through rate on Hot Home badges over time
options.feedback_short: CTR decline — visible but too late.
options.feedback_reveal: Declining CTR is real but lagging. By the time aggregate CTR drops, trust has been eroding for weeks. Users don't stop clicking all at once — they stop trusting gradually and the aggregate only catches it after the damage is done.

options.label: The gap between how fast Hot Home listings sell vs non-Hot listings is narrowing
options.feedback_short: Narrowing accuracy gap — strong leading indicator.
options.feedback_reveal: If Hot Home listings are selling only marginally faster than non-Hot listings, the prediction is losing its edge. The gap in days-to-offer between Hot and non-Hot homes is a leading indicator of whether the feature still has informational value — and it shows up before CTR changes.

options.label: Repeat user behaviour — buyers who previously acted on Hot Home badges are now ignoring them
options.feedback_short: Repeat trust decay — the earliest and most accurate signal.
options.feedback_reveal: A buyer who acted on a Hot Home badge last month and got burned will not act on the next one. Cohort-level repeat action rate — users who previously engaged and whether they engage again — detects trust erosion before it appears in any aggregate metric. This is the canary in the coal mine.

calibration.strong: You're thinking at senior or staff level consistently. The next step is connecting these metrics into a decision framework — not just measuring, but defining what threshold in each metric should trigger a product change.
calibration.developing: Good instincts. The reframe that unlocks senior-level thinking: always ask what the feature claims before asking how to measure it. The claim defines the measurement.
calibration.early: You're building the right foundation. Focus on this pattern: prediction features require accuracy metrics first, engagement metrics second. Engagement without accuracy measures the wrong thing.

episodes.title: The Three Metrics Redfin's Hot Home Actually Needs
episodes.subtitle: Accuracy, utility, and trust — why you need all three and why most measurement frameworks only get one.
episodes.youtube_description: A prediction feature has three independent success conditions: the prediction is accurate, it drives value-creating behaviour, and it builds lasting trust. Most dashboards track one. This episode shows you how to design for all three.

episodes.title: What to Build First When the Hot Home Badge Is Failing
episodes.subtitle: The explicit call most candidates avoid — what to instrument first and what to cut.
episodes.youtube_description: You've diagnosed the problem. Now what do you actually build? This episode walks through the prioritisation decision for a prediction feature — what to instrument first, what to cut, and the framework that works across any recommendation or prediction product.
